Chapter 16
Alcohols, Diols, and Thiols

Section 16.1
SOURCES OF ALCOHOLS

Sources of Alcohols
Methanol ( CH 3 OH ) is a byproduct in the production of charcoal from wood; nowadays, it is produced mostly by reduction of CO
Natural alcohols are produced during the fermentation process
Ethanol and isopropanol are produced from petroleum via hydration of ethylene and propene
Many reactions we have encountered previously yield alcohols as products !
Reactions that Yield Alcohols
Reactions That Yield Alcohols-1
Reactions That Yield Alcohols-2

Section 16.2
PREPARATION OF ALCOHOLS BY REDUCTION OF ALDEHYDES AND KETONES

Reduction via Hydrogenation
Heterogeneous metal catalysts and hydrogen gas reduce R 2 C=O to R 2 HCOH
Metals used include Pt, Pd, Ni, or Ru

Reduction via Metal Hydrides
Laboratory reductions of carbonyl compounds use metal hydrides ( H − ) followed by acidic workup ( H + ) to add H 2 across C=O
Sodium borohydride is mild and works in water or alcohols
These reactions do not involve H 2 , but instead the sequential addition of H − and H + .

Reduction via Metal Hydrides-1
Laboratory reductions of carbonyl compounds use metal hydrides ( H − ) followed by acidic workup ( H + ) to add H 2 across C=O
Lithium aluminum hydride (LAH) reacts violently with water and alcohols and requires strictly aprotic conditions

Mechanisms of Metal Hydride Reduction
Note that the new C–H comes from the metal hydride; the new O–H comes from the workup acid (typically water)

Section 16.3
PREPARATION OF ALCOHOLS BY REDUCTION OF CARBOXYLIC ACIDS

Reduction of Carboxylic Acids
If H − could displace OH − in a carboxylic acid, – CO 2 H could be reduced to CH 2 OH , a primary alcohol
Only the strongest reducing agents (LAH) can accomplish this
NaBH 4 does not work!

Section 16.4
PREPARATION OF ALCOHOLS FROM EPOXIDES

Opening of Epoxides
The alcohol product contains two new carbons relative to the starting alkyl halide (precursor to the nucleophile)

Opening of Epoxides-1
C–O bonds in epoxides are readily cleaved by carbon nucleophiles (RLi and RMgX) and other nucleophiles via S N 2
Other epoxides may be used to yield secondary and tertiary alcohols, but regioselectivity issues exist (more in Chapter 17 )

Section 16.5
PREPARATION OF DIOLS

Reduction of Dicarbonyls
Dicarbonyl compounds are reduced under hydrogenation or metal hydride reduction conditions to yield diols
Note the nomenclature of the diol product
Pentanediol
Two locants are necessary to locate the –OH groups

Dihydroxylation Yields Vicinal Diols
Alkenes react with Os O 4 to give cyclic osmate esters …
…which are cleaved in the presence of an oxidant and base to give vicinal diols

Dihydroxylation Yields Vicinal Diols-1
Alkenes react with Os O 4 to give cyclic osmate esters …
…which are cleaved in the presence of an oxidant and base to give vicinal diols
This reaction is catalytic! Nice !

Dihydroxylation Yields Vicinal Diols-2
Simply adding the alkene with Os O 4 , oxidant, and base gives the product after several hours
Both bonds to Os O 4 are made simultaneously, so dihydroxylation is stereospecifically syn
In the presence of a chiral base catalyst, the reaction is highly enantioselective as well
